content.problemStatement:
Chef and his friends would like to attend a large New Year's party in Goa. However, their teacher has given them an assignment to complete. Chef asked for your help to complete the assignment quickly so that he can attend the party with complete freedom.

Chef was given an array A=[A1,A2,‚Ä¶,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč] of length NNN, an array B=[B1,B2,‚Ä¶,BM]B = [B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_M]B=[B1‚Äč,B2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,BM‚Äč] of length MMM, and a prime number PPP. His task is to find the number of **good pairs**. An ordered pair of integers (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) is said to be a **good pair** if it satisfies the following conditions:


1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \leq i \leq N1‚Č§i‚Č§N
1‚Č§j‚Č§M1 \leq j \leq M1‚Č§j‚Č§M
(Ai‚äēBj)<P(A_i \oplus B_j) \lt P(Ai‚Äč‚äēBj‚Äč)<P
PPP divides ((Ai‚čÖ(Ai‚äēBj))‚ąí1)((A_i \cdot (A_i \oplus B_j)) - 1)((Ai‚Äč‚čÖ(Ai‚Äč‚äēBj‚Äč))‚ąí1)
where ‚äē\oplus‚äē denotes the bitwise XOR operation.

Please help Chef and his friends, and find the number of **good pairs**.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers N,MN, MN,M, and PPP, respectively.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč.
The third line of each test case contains MMM space-separated integers B1,B2,‚Ä¶,BMB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_MB1‚Äč,B2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,BM‚Äč.


Output Format
For each test case, output one line containing a single integer ‚ÄĒ the number of **good pairs**.
constraints: 1 ‚Č§ N, M ‚Č§ 2¬∑10^5 (sum of N and sum of M over all test cases ‚Č§ 2¬∑10^5)
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains three space‚ÄĎseparated integers N, M and P (P is a prime).
- The second line contains N space‚ÄĎseparated integers A1 ‚Ä¶ AN.
- The third line contains M space‚ÄĎseparated integers B1 ‚Ä¶ BM.
All numbers satisfy the constraints given in the statement.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of good ordered pairs (i, j).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111: There are 444 ordered pairs of indices. Looking at them individually,

(1,1)(1,1)(1,1) is a good pair because (7‚äē2)=5<17(7\oplus 2) = 5 \lt 17(7‚äē2)=5<17, and ((7‚čÖ(7‚äē2))‚ąí1)=34((7 \cdot (7 \oplus 2)) - 1) = 34((7‚čÖ(7‚äē2))‚ąí1)=34 is divisible by 171717.
(1,2)(1, 2)(1,2) is not a good pair because ((7‚čÖ(7‚äē13))‚ąí1)=69((7 \cdot (7 \oplus 13)) - 1) = 69((7‚čÖ(7‚äē13))‚ąí1)=69 is not divisible by 171717.
(2,1)(2, 1)(2,1) is not a good pair because ((14‚čÖ(14‚äē2))‚ąí1)=167((14 \cdot (14 \oplus 2)) - 1) = 167((14‚čÖ(14‚äē2))‚ąí1)=167 is not divisible by 171717.
(2,2)(2, 2)(2,2) is not a good pair because ((14‚čÖ(14‚äē13))‚ąí1)=41((14 \cdot (14 \oplus 13)) - 1) = 41((14‚čÖ(14‚äē13))‚ąí1)=41 is not divisible by 171717.

Test Case 222: The only ordered pair of indices is (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1). This is not a good pair because ((2‚čÖ(2‚äē2))‚ąí1)=‚ąí1((2 \cdot (2 \oplus 2)) - 1) = -1((2‚čÖ(2‚äē2))‚ąí1)=‚ąí1 is not divisible by 111111, since it leaves a remainder of 101010.

solution.approach:
For a pair (i, j) to be good we need:
1. (Ai xor Bj) < P
2. P | (Ai¬∑(Ai xor Bj) ‚Äď 1)
Because P is prime, condition‚ÄĮ2 is equivalent to Ai¬∑X ‚Č° 1 (mod P) where X = Ai xor Bj. If Ai is divisible by P there is no X satisfying the congruence, so such Ai never contributes.
Otherwise Ai has a unique modular inverse inv = Ai^{-1} (mod P) with 1 ‚Č§ inv ‚Č§ P‚ÄĎ1. The congruence forces X = inv. Hence the pair is good iff
   Ai xor Bj = inv   ‚áĒ   Bj = Ai xor inv.
Since inv < P, condition‚ÄĮ1 is automatically satisfied.
Therefore the problem reduces to:
- Build a frequency map of all values in B.
- For each Ai with Ai % P ‚Č† 0 compute inv = pow(Ai, P‚ÄĎ2, P) (Fermat's little theorem).
- Let need = Ai xor inv. Add freq[need] to the answer.
The algorithm runs in O(N + M) per test case (plus O(log‚ÄĮP) for each modular exponentiation) and uses O(M) extra memory for the frequency map.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N¬∑log‚ÄĮP + M) per test case (log‚ÄĮP ‚Č§ 30 because P ‚Č§ 10^9).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M) for the frequency map of array B.
